document:
The editing and refresh logic in linenoise is responsible for keeping the internal buffer, cursor position, and terminal display synchronized. The central state object is `struct linenoiseState`, which contains the editable buffer, buffer length, prompt, prompt length, current cursor position, previous cursor position, current line length, terminal column count, multiline refresh state, and history index. Editing functions mutate this state, then call refresh functions to redraw the prompt and buffer on screen.

Insertion is handled by `linenoiseEditInsert`. If the cursor is at the end of the line and the new text fits, linenoise can sometimes avoid a full redraw by writing the inserted bytes directly to the terminal. This fast path is used only in a simple single-line case where the prompt plus buffer still fits within the terminal width and no hints are active. If mask mode is enabled, it writes an asterisk instead of the real character. If the cursor is in the middle of the line, insertion shifts the tail of the buffer with `memmove`, writes the new bytes into place, updates length and cursor position, and refreshes the line.

Cursor movement is UTF-8-aware. Moving left uses the byte length of the previous UTF-8 character or grapheme cluster; moving right uses the length of the next one. Home and End move to byte position zero or to the current line length. Deletion is also UTF-8-aware. Delete removes the character at the cursor without changing the cursor position. Backspace removes the character before the cursor and moves the cursor left. Ctrl-W deletes the previous word by scanning backward over spaces and non-space characters. Ctrl-U clears the whole line, while Ctrl-K deletes from the cursor to the end of the line.

Refresh is necessary because terminal display width is not the same as byte length. Linenoise includes helpers for UTF-8 decoding, grapheme cluster movement, and approximate display width. It treats ANSI prompt escape sequences as zero-width, so colored prompts can be drawn without confusing cursor calculations. This is important for prompts such as the example program's ANSI-colored prompt, where the raw string contains escape bytes that should not count as visible columns.

The library has both single-line and multiline refresh modes. Single-line refresh redraws the prompt and current buffer within one terminal row. Multiline mode tracks how many rows the previous render used, clears the old render, and redraws the new one across rows as needed. The public `linenoiseSetMultiLine` utility switches multiline editing on or off. The implementation uses a small set of escape sequences for cursor movement and erasing, accepting some possible flicker in exchange for simplicity and compatibility.

Refresh and editing therefore form the visual core of linenoise. The editing functions update the logical line buffer, while refresh functions translate that state into terminal output that keeps the prompt, typed text, hints, completions, cursor movement, and multiline behavior coherent.
